title: Split Industrial Coffee Table
summary: Two mirrored 2x2 frames with mitered 1x8 tops that sit side by side with a deliberate gap down the middle — an industrial look built from nothing but construction lumber.
description:
The "split" in the name is the whole design idea: rather than one coffee table, you build two identical three-legged frames as mirror images of each other, and set them down side by side so their mitered tops read as one surface with an intentional seam running through it. That seam is the entire visual hook, so the two frames need to actually be mirror images — not just "close" — or the gap reads as a mistake instead of a design choice.

The one genuinely tricky cut is the diagonal center brace: it's ripped from a 2x4 at a 45° bevel down the middle, then each resulting half is ripped again down to 1-1/2" wide. That's two rip cuts at odd angles on a small piece of stock — go slow, use a push stick, and don't try to freehand it on a table saw without a fence set precisely. Everything else is pocket-hole leg-and-stretcher construction, the same skills as any basic table base.

tools.note: For the 45° corner miters on the 1x8 top boards.
For ripping the diagonal center brace out of a 2x4 at a 45° bevel, then ripping the halves down to width. Go slow and use a push stick.

materials.name: Pine, 1x8, 12 ft
materials.note: Mitered top boards for both frame halves.

materials.name: Pine, 2x4 scrap
materials.note: For the diagonal center brace — ripped down.

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 45°, ends not parallel — long point to long point.
Ripped from a 2x4 at a 45° bevel down the middle, then each half ripped again to 1-1/2" wide.
Both ends cut parallel at 45°, long-point-to-short-point measurement.
Both ends cut perpendicular at 45°, long point to long point.
Both ends cut perpendicular at 45°; ripped narrower than full 1x8 width.

steps.title: Build the first corner
steps.body: Drill 1-1/2" pocket holes on each end of the short leg stretcher. Attach to two legs with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws and glue — this becomes one corner of the first frame.

steps.title: Add the second and third legs
steps.body: Drill pocket holes on both ends of the mid stretcher, then attach one end to the corner from the previous step. Attach a third leg to the open end.

steps.title: Close the frame with the long stretcher
steps.body:
Drill a pocket hole on one end of the long stretcher and attach it to the third leg with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws and glue, closing the three-legged frame.

Repeat this whole sequence for the second frame as an exact mirror image of the first — the split-table look depends on the two frames actually mirroring each other, not just being similar.

steps.title: Add the angled corner braces
steps.body: Cut the two angled corner braces from scrap 2x2 at 45°, drill pocket holes as marked, and attach.

steps.title: Rip and install the diagonal center brace
steps.body: Rip a 2x4 length down the middle at a 45° bevel on the table saw, then rip each resulting half down to 1-1/2" wide. Go slow with a push stick — this is the one operation on the build that isn't a simple square cut. Attach the finished brace with 2" screws and glue.

steps.title: Attach the mitered tops
steps.body: Attach each frame's mitered top boards from underneath with 2" screws. Make sure both tops are mirrored to each other so the two frames sit together as one surface with the seam running straight down the middle.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand all surfaces and edges, especially the mitered top joints. Finish both frames identically so the pair reads as one piece.

images.alt: Split industrial-style coffee table with mitered top seam
